title: NICE 2025 Falls Guidelines: What Changed and What You Need to Do
description: What changed in the NICE 2025 falls guidelines (NG249). Key updates from CG161 and what your organization needs to do.

blocks.question: What changed in NICE 2025 falls guidelines compared to 2013?
blocks.directAnswer: NICE NG249 2025 explicitly advises against standalone predictive tools (like Timed Up and Go alone). It mandates multifactorial assessment for anyone with falls history, unexplained falls, or frailty. New high-risk criteria: 2 or more falls in past year, any unexplained fall, or evidence of frailty.

tldrBullets.text: Single screening tools are no longer recommended for falls prevention decisions
Multifactorial assessment is now mandatory, not optional
Three new high-risk criteria trigger formal comprehensive assessment

blocks.expandedAnswer:
The 2013 guideline (CG161) introduced multifactorial assessment as best practice. The 2025 update (NG249) makes it mandatory. This reflects accumulating evidence that single tests miss significant risk.

Old approach (CG161): Use screening tools to identify at-risk people, then perform multifactorial assessment if screening positive. New approach (NG249): Ask two brief questions. If positive or if frailty indicators present, perform comprehensive multifactorial assessment. Skip the screening step.

The 2025 update also defines high-risk criteria more precisely. Previously, "recurrent falls" was vague. Now it's specific: two or more falls in the past 12 months. Unexplained falls (syncope, collapse) are considered high-risk even if infrequent. Frailty, measured via Clinical Frailty Scale or FRAIL scale, is recognized as a risk factor warranting formal assessment.

This shift reflects clinical reality: many falls result from complex interactions that no single test can capture. Compliance with NG249 2025 means embedding multifactorial assessment into your routine practice, documenting findings systematically, and acting on results.

sources.title: NICE Guideline NG249: Falls prevention in older people (2025 update)
NICE Guideline CG161: Falls prevention in older people (previous guidance)
Clinical Frailty Scale: A new tool for identifying vulnerable older people

blocks.body:
What changed: from screening to direct assessment

NICE CG161 (2013) recommended a two-stage process: first screen with tools (Timed Up and Go, Falls Risk Assessment Tool), then assess multifactorially if screening suggested risk. Research showed this approach left gaps. Some high-risk residents scored well on single tests. Others with normal scores experienced falls.

NG249 (2025) removes the screening stage. Instead, it recommends starting with two simple questions: "Have you had a fall in the past year?" and "Do you feel unsteady?" Positive answers trigger comprehensive assessment immediately. This is more efficient: you don't waste time screening low-risk people, and you don't miss high-risk people hiding behind good test scores.

The practical effect: your team should ask these two questions routinely during health reviews or when someone first enters your care setting. Document the answers. If either is yes, or if you observe frailty indicators, proceed to multifactorial assessment. This simplicity reduces administrative burden while improving safety.

Three new high-risk criteria (NG249 2025)

First: Two or more falls in the past 12 months. This replaces the vaguer "recurrent falls" from older guidance. Be precise: document each fall, including date, mechanism, and outcome. A log helps you recognize when someone crosses the two-fall threshold.

Second: Any unexplained fall or syncope. This includes collapses, faints, or falls the resident cannot explain. These suggest cardiac arrhythmia, postural hypotension, or neurological issues. Unlike slips and trips (environmental), unexplained falls indicate intrinsic medical problems needing investigation. Refer to geriatrics or cardiology as appropriate.

Third: Evidence of frailty. NICE specifies two tools: the Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) or the FRAIL scale. The CFS rates function on a 1-9 scale, with scores of 5 or higher indicating frailty. The FRAIL scale assesses fatigue, resistance, ambulation, illness, and loss of weight. Either tool can identify vulnerable residents warranting formal assessment. Staff training is essential; frailty assessment is not difficult but requires consistency.

Compliance steps for your organization

Step 1: Audit current practice. Review five recent falls assessments. Do they address all four domains (function, medication, medical history, environment)? Are they documented clearly? Many organizations discover gaps: medication reviews are missing, environmental assessment is superficial. Identify your gaps.

Step 2: Choose assessment tools or platforms. You can build your own multifactorial assessment form or use existing tools (STRATIFY, Falls Risk Assessment Tool, Downton Scale, or digital platforms like Able Assess). Whatever you choose must be systematic and comprehensive. Ensure staff training is included.

Step 3: Implement the two-question screen. Train all staff who conduct health reviews (nurses, primary care physicians, care coordinators) to ask these questions routinely and document answers. Make it part of your electronic record system if you have one.

Step 4: Define your multifactorial assessment pathway. Who performs assessments? When? What's the timeline? In a large care home, a designated physical therapist or nurse might lead. In general practice, the primary care physician or practice nurse might assess. Document the pathway clearly so staff know what happens next.

Step 5: Review and update your care planning process. Ensure assessment results trigger documented action. Document intervention decisions (what will be done, who is responsible, by when). Review at agreed intervals.

Step 6: Audit compliance quarterly. Sample 10-15 residents with documented falls risk. Check: Was assessment done? Is it multifactorial? Are results documented? Is a care plan in place? Track compliance metrics and feed back to staff. This creates accountability.

How Able Assess aligns with NG249 2025

Able Assess embeds the NG249 2025 requirements into a structured digital workflow. The platform opens with the two screening questions. Positive answers or detected frailty trigger a comprehensive multifactorial assessment module.

The assessment covers all four required domains systematically: functional assessment (gait, balance, strength, assessed through validated questions and optional video analysis), medication review (cross-checking current medications against a database of fall-risk compounds), medical history (falls history, relevant diagnoses, syncope events), and environmental scan (specific guidance on hazards by room).

Results are presented clearly: risk level (low, moderate, high) with domain-specific findings. Rather than generic recommendations, Able Assess identifies which domains require intervention. For example: "Balance is compromised; consider physical therapy" and "On two psychoactive medications; recommend primary care physician review."

Documentation is systematic and time-stamped. Your compliance audit becomes easier: you can generate reports showing which residents have been assessed, when, and with what results. This transparency supports both safety and regulatory scrutiny.

stats.stat: Single screening tools correctly identify only 40-60% of at-risk community-dwelling older adults
Clinical Frailty Scale score 5+ has 85% sensitivity for adverse outcomes including falls
Multifactorial interventions based on comprehensive assessment reduce fall incidence by 32-40%
stats.source: NICE NG249 meta-analysis of intervention studies

items.question: Do I have to stop using Timed Up and Go?
items.answer: NICE 2025 doesn't ban TUG. It advises against using it in isolation. TUG remains useful as one component of functional assessment. However, don't rely on a single TUG score to make fall prevention decisions. Combine it with other assessments, medication review, and environmental evaluation.

items.question: Is the two-question screen sufficient for initial assessment?
items.answer: The two questions (falls in past year, unsteadiness) are screening, not comprehensive assessment. Positive answers trigger full multifactorial assessment. Low-risk residents still need documented reasoning: "No falls history, no unsteadiness, no frailty indicators; general health promotion recommended." Document this decision.

items.question: What if someone doesn't fit the high-risk criteria but I'm worried?
items.answer: Clinical judgment matters. If you're concerned despite low-risk scores, you can perform comprehensive assessment anyway. Document your clinical reasoning. NICE guidance supports this flexibility: "Consider assessment if clinically indicated." Safety concerns should drive assessment.

items.question: How often should we reassess?
items.answer: NICE 2025 recommends reassessment if there has been a fall, hospitalization, new medication, or change in function. Minimum annual reassessment is advised for all older people at risk. Homes should have a system flagging when reassessment is due.

items.question: Do we need separate documentation for NG249 compliance?
items.answer: No. Integrate NG249 documentation into your standard assessment and care planning forms. Record screening answers, multifactorial assessment findings (organized by domain), risk level, interventions planned, and review dates. A well-designed form serves both safety and compliance.

blocks.heading: Meet NG249 2025 requirements systematically
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess is designed to NG249 2025 specifications, guiding your team through screening, comprehensive assessment, and documentation to ensure compliance.
